tldr: Introduces a `scheduling_mode` column on `transitions` and routes the advance endpoint through it. The new mode controls whether a transition runs immediately or is queued for later.

groups.title: Data model: scheduling_mode on transitions
groups.intro: Before reading the endpoint changes, anchor on the model. A new `scheduling_mode` column lands on `transitions` with three states. The advance endpoint downstream branches on this value, so it pays to internalise the mode set first.

diagrams.title: transitions (after this PR)
diagrams.source:
erDiagram
    TRANSITIONS {
        uuid id PK
        uuid entity_id FK
        string from_state
        string to_state
        string scheduling_mode
        timestamp scheduled_for
    }

lines.content: ADD COLUMN scheduling_mode TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'immediate'
CREATE INDEX idx_transitions_scheduled_for ON transitions (scheduled_for) WHERE scheduled_for IS NOT NULL;

annotations.title: Default 'immediate' preserves existing behaviour
annotations.body: Every existing row will read `scheduling_mode = 'immediate'` after this migration, which is also what every existing call site expects when the column is absent. The downstream endpoint's branching keeps the immediate path identical to the old behaviour — this is the backwards-compat seam.

groups.title: Entry point: POST /transitions/:id/advance
groups.intro: The reviewer's mental model from group 1 (immediate/queued/deferred) lands here. The handler now branches on `scheduling_mode` and dispatches accordingly. Pay attention to the queued path — it's the only one that writes to `scheduled_for`.

lines.content: const transition = await db.transitions.findOne({ id: req.params.id })

annotations.title: Branch maps 1:1 to the mode enum
annotations.body: The switch covers all three values from the migration's CHECK constraint. If a fourth mode is ever added, both this switch and the migration's CHECK need to move in lockstep — worth flagging in a test.

annotations.title: What schedules a 'deferred' transition?
annotations.body: The `deferred` case returns 202 without writing `scheduled_for` or enqueueing. Who's responsible for eventually picking these up? If nothing schedules them, this is effectively a black hole.

annotations.title: Newly exported internals
annotations.body: `runTransition` and `enqueueTransition` are now exported from this module. Check whether the export is used (tests? other handlers?) or whether it can drop.

annotations.title: Scope doc promised an `advance_at` field
annotations.body: The scope doc (`docs/transition-scheduling.md`) describes the request body as `{ advance_at: ISO8601 }`, but the handler reads `req.body?.scheduled_for`. One of them is wrong.

suggestions.body: Consider adding a default arm that 500s on unknown modes. Right now an unexpected value silently falls through the switch and returns whatever the response was on the previous statement.

diagrams.title: Advance flow by scheduling mode
diagrams.source:
flowchart LR
  client[Client] -->|POST /advance| handler[advance handler]
  handler -->|immediate| run[runTransition]
  handler -->|queued| enq[enqueueTransition]
  handler -->|deferred| nop[no-op 202]
  run --> done[204]
  enq --> done2[202]

reviewPrompts: Does the migration's CHECK constraint stay in sync with the handler's switch arms?
Is there a story for 'deferred' transitions actually running?
